explicit_text: I was born in Martinsville, Virginia, which is located in Henry County just across the line from North Carolina. It was my early home before we moved due to my father's financial difficulties. Martinsville was part of my southern roots and family history.

explicit_text: My maternal grandfather was the manager of the John Daniel Plantation, which was partly in Amherst County near Sweet Briar College and also in Campbell County near Lynchburg. This plantation was a significant part of my family background and southern heritage. It connected me to the land and history of Virginia.

explicit_text: On my father's side, the Kester family lived around Cologne in Germany. They were tailors by trade, a profession that had been in the family for generations. My father wanted to be a mining engineer, but his father insisted he continue the tailoring tradition established in Cologne.

explicit_text: My ancestors, who were Quakers, left Germany and went to England where they connected with William Penn. Penn encouraged them to survey parts of Pennsylvania, and they were given land there, including areas near Philadelphia. This connection to William Penn is a proud part of my family's Quaker heritage.

explicit_text: Philadelphia holds significance for my family because a plaque there commemorates my ancestor Praetorius, who signed the first written protest against slavery in America. It is a place that symbolizes my family's Quaker heritage and their early stand for justice. I learned about this only years after growing up.

explicit_text: My father was a member of the Ku Klux Klan, though not a leader. My mother discovered his Klan regalia, which nearly broke her heart. Despite this, my father was respected in the community and did not engage in violence, but his membership was a complicated part of my family history.

explicit_text: I attended Lynchburg College where I earned my A.B. degree and certificates in Bible and Greek, preparing to be a preacher. The Dean was supportive enough to allow me to take two months off for a speaking tour in Europe. Lynchburg College was central to my education and early activism.

explicit_text: In 1923, through the YMCA and the World Student Christian Federation, I was selected to visit war-torn Europe to raise support for European students. David Porter, the National Secretary of the Student YMCA, asked me to be the Regional Director of European Student Relief in the South. The YMCA played a key role in launching my pacifist activism and international work.

The National Secretary of the Student branch of that organization asked me to be the Regional Director of European Student Relief in the South. This organization played a key role in launching my pacifist activism and international work.

explicit_text: During my two-month speaking tour in 1923, I was a guest of the French government and visited places like Verdun to see the battlefields. The experience of witnessing the devastation of war in France deeply influenced my decision to become a pacifist. France was a key location in my early activism and education about war's horrors.

explicit_text: Verdun was one of the battlefields in France that I visited during my 1923 trip through Europe. I was taken around by the same guide who had shown Woodrow Wilson the battlefield. Seeing the devastation firsthand at Verdun convinced me that I wanted nothing to do with war.

Their encouragement led me to engage with both white and Negro colleges in the South. Tobias was a key figure in promoting interracial understanding in my work.
